name: My commute turned into a work trip and I crashed—what now in Utah?

mainEntity.name: My commute turned into a work trip and I crashed—what now in Utah?

acceptedAnswer.text: Standard commutes aren't usually covered, but if you were performing a 'special errand' or traveling between job sites, you are likely eligible for benefits. A lawyer can help determine if your specific situation qualifies as an exception to the 'going and coming' rule.
acceptedAnswer.description:
Utah law typically follows the 'going and coming' rule, which excludes your standard commute from workers' comp coverage. However, if your commute transformed into a work trip—perhaps because you stopped to pick up supplies, were on a multi-destination route, or were 'on-call'—you may be eligible. If your employer provides a company vehicle for the commute as an integral part of the job, the coverage window often widens.

Exceptions to the rule include:
- Special errands performed at the employer's request.
- Traveling between different work sites.
- Using a vehicle that serves as a 'mobile office.'

Determining ‘course of employment’ during a commute is legally complex and highly fact-specific. If your claim was denied because it happened during your commute, a legal review of your specific job requirements is necessary to challenge the insurer's decision.
